name: Zinc
overLimitRisk: Chronic intake above this level is associated with copper deficiency, which can in turn cause anemia and immune dysfunction.
note: Iron & calcium compete for absorption; sustained high intake depletes copper

name: Copper
timing: With food, away from high-dose zinc
note: Depleted by sustained high zinc intake

name: Iron
note: Strongly competes with zinc for absorption

name: Calcium
timing: Split doses, away from zinc & iron
note: Competes with zinc/iron/magnesium for absorption

name: Magnesium
note: May aid sleep; competes with zinc/calcium for absorption

name: Boron
note: Interacts with mineral metabolism (magnesium, calcium)

name: Vitamin D
note: Works with K2 to direct calcium to bone
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin D Fact Sheet

name: Vitamin K
timing: With a fat-containing meal, ideally alongside vitamin D
note: Commonly paired with vitamin D; interacts with blood thinners
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin K Fact Sheet

name: Vitamin E
overLimitRisk: High doses may increase bleeding risk by affecting blood clotting, particularly in combination with blood-thinning medication.
note: Fat-soluble; high doses may affect blood clotting
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin E Fact Sheet

name: Vitamin A
overLimitRisk: Vitamin A is fat-soluble and accumulates in the liver over time; chronic excess intake has been associated with liver damage.
note: Fat-soluble; accumulates in liver over time
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin A Fact Sheet

name: Fish oil (EPA/DHA)
note: Absorbs better with dietary fat present

name: Biotin
upperLimit: no established UL; interferes with lab assays
timing: Any time; pause 3-5 days before bloodwork
note: Can distort thyroid/troponin immunoassay lab results

name: Vitamin B12
note: Often redundant if a multivitamin already includes it; water-soluble, excess excreted
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin B12 Fact Sheet

name: Folate/Folic acid
overLimitRisk: High folic acid intake can mask the blood-test signs of vitamin B12 deficiency, potentially delaying diagnosis of an underlying deficiency.
note: High intake can mask B12 deficiency symptoms

name: Multivitamin
note: Often already contains B12, some copper, zinc - check label for overlap

name: Probiotic
timing: Empty stomach or right before a meal, apart from other supplements
note: Large supplement loads at the same time may reduce benefit

name: Ashwagandha
upperLimit: no established UL; cycling commonly recommended
note: May affect thyroid hormone levels with continuous long-term use

name: Apigenin
upperLimit: no established UL - limited long-term data
note: Long-term human safety data is limited

name: Melatonin
upperLimit: no formal UL; commonly 0.5-5mg effective range
note: Higher doses aren't necessarily more effective; can cause grogginess

name: Creatine monohydrate
timing: Any time; consistency matters more than timing
note: One of the most studied supplements; timing relative to workout has minimal effect

name: Vitamin C
timing: Any time, can enhance iron absorption if taken together
note: Water-soluble, excess excreted
sourceHint: NIH ODS Vitamin C Fact Sheet
